data.title: What Chinese phrase ‘wearing small shoes’ means and its connection to painful bridal shoes
data.description:
In modern-day office politics, Chinese people use the phrase “wearing small shoes” to describe someone who abuses their power or position to deliberately make things difficult for others.
The term originates from an ancient marriage folklore in which the bride was forced to wear embroidered shoes that were intentionally made too small to torment her.
Shoes hold a deep significance in Chinese marriages and wedding ceremonies. A pair represents a couple staying together in harmony, symbolising companionship and a lifelong union.

A common Chinese wedding tradition involves hiding the bride’s wedding shoes and having the groom, often helped by bridesmaids, search for them.
This playful ritual adds joy to the ceremony while testing the groom’s patience. It also symbolises his dedication and commitment to the bride.
However, the idiom “wearing small shoes” has a more sombre historical context.
The literal “small shoes” refer to the intricately embroidered footwear worn by women who had undergone foot binding in ancient times, causing immense physical suffering.

At that time, marriages were not decided by the couple themselves but arranged by parents and facilitated by matchmakers.
The matchmakers would often request a sample of the bride’s shoes to confirm whether her feet were bound.
If both families agreed to the match, the groom’s family would craft a pair of embroidered shoes based on the sample of her size and send them to the bride’s home along with the engagement gifts.

On her wedding day, the bride was expected to wear these shoes as she entered her new in-laws’ home.
If the embroidered shoes are intentionally made too small, causing the bride discomfort, it is a deliberate attempt to make things difficult for her.
The specific origin that transformed this ritual into the modern idiom can be traced back to a folk tale from the Northern Song dynasty (960–1279).
A woman called Qiaoyu refused to be married off by her wicked stepmother to an undesirable suitor, who is an ugly, mute yet wealthy man.
When a matchmaker later introduced a promising young scholar as a potential husband, the stepmother secretly plotted her revenge.

She intentionally cut a much smaller shoe sample and handed it to the matchmaker to deliver to the groom’s family, hoping to sabotage the match.
On her wedding day, Qiaoyu could not fit her feet into the tiny embroidered shoes, preventing her from boarding the sedan chair and heading to the groom’s home.
Overwhelmed by shame, distress and despair, she took her own life.
Over time, it evolved into the idiom “wearing small shoes”, which is now widely used to describe someone abusing their power or position to humiliate others.
The idiom is especially common in the workplace and typically refers to superiors targeting subordinates by delaying approvals, assigning excessively heavy tasks or rejecting work proposals.
It may also involve salary cuts, demotions or corporate abuse, such as coercing an employee to resign.

data.title: Reinventing Kaohsiung: Taiwan’s port city transcends its industrial past
data.description:
Few cities have managed to shake off an industrial past as thoroughly as Kaohsiung. Once defined by its shipyards, heavy industry and petrochemicals, Taiwan’s third largest city has undergone a transformation that is nothing short of remarkable.
In the last two decades, Kaohsiung has become a green, services-oriented cultural city. While it lacks the scale, resources and iconic urban attractions of Tokyo, Hong Kong, Singapore or Dubai, the home of Taiwan’s second busiest airport exudes a distinctive small-city charm.
Here, greenery and culture intertwine, as state-of-the-art infrastructure coexists with bustling traditional markets and historic neighbourhoods.

Kaohsiung’s transformation shows that city planners, working in concert with the government, civil society and local communities, can turn a drab industrial city into a smart, green urban centre – despite the sharp polarisation that has characterised Taiwanese politics for the past three decades.
Since Taiwan’s democratisation in the 1990s, Kaohsiung’s mayoral elections have typically favoured the Democratic Progressive Party. But this has seldom prevented productive collaboration with ministries in Taipei, even when a Kuomintang government was in charge.
Kaohsiung’s transformation holds useful lessons for secondary cities across the region grappling with the decline of traditional industries while trying to meet citizens’ demands for greener, more liveable urban spaces.

Reimagining the waterfront
Perhaps the most striking expression of Kaohsiung’s renewal is the redevelopment of its old port area into an aesthetically compelling cultural district.
As the port lost ground to counterparts in mainland China in the late 1990s, the city took the opportunity to reimagine its waterfront. Redevelopment saw the port authority transfer five piers in the Love River bay to the city, allowing for their transformation.
Today, these piers house the Pier-2 Art Centre, home to museums and performance space; the Kaohsiung Music Centre; a whale-shaped port terminal; and a yacht marina. Nearby, the Hamasen area has also had a facelift and now boasts boutique hotels, cafes, restaurants and local craft shops, many occupying carefully restored colonial-era shophouses. Meanwhile, much of the Love River itself has been converted into a riverside “green corridor”, ideal for cyclists, runners and walkers alike.
The Pier-2 Art Centre is easily accessed via the Hamasen mass rapid transit (MRT) and light rail transit (LRT) stations.

Investing in public transport
The second driver of Kaohsiung’s transformation has been significant investment in public transport, particularly the two MRT lines launched in 2008 and the Circular LRT, which opened in 2015 and was completed just last year. The LRT forms a loop around the city’s core, in many sections running on “green tracks” near dense, historic neighbourhoods – enhancing its visual appeal.
Some stations have become landmarks in themselves. The futuristic Kaohsiung Main Station and the whimsical Formosa Boulevard Station show how public transport infrastructure can be both functional and beautiful. The latter, home to the celebrated Dome of Light stained-glass installation by Italian artist Narcissus Quagliata, has become a tourist attraction in its own right. Nestled between these stations lies the old Liuhe Night Market, one of the most popular in Taiwan.
While Kaohsiung still experiences peak-hour congestion (partly due to the prevalence of motorcycles) and public transit use is lower than city planners would like, it is a vast improvement on the chronic traffic jams and pollution of 25 years ago.

Greening the city
The third catalyst for Kaohsiung’s transformation has been a sustained commitment to urban greening – not only through new urban parks, but also by integrating small-scale urban greenery into both existing and new neighbourhoods.
Among the new parks, Weiwuying Metropolitan Park is probably the most impressive. Civic groups began lobbying in the late 1990s to convert an old military base in east Kaohsiung into an ecological park. Their efforts bore fruit with the park’s completion in 2010. Today, it is beloved by residents for leisure, exercise and large-scale outdoor events, and also preserves a stretch of the old army barracks as a reminder of its unique heritage.
The park encircles Taiwan’s largest cultural venue, the National Kaohsiung Centre for the Arts, completed in 2014 and once described by The Guardian newspaper as the “biggest arts venue on Earth”.

Despite its size, the centre feels welcoming, even intimate, thanks to architect Francine Houben's semi-open design. Inspired by a banyan grove nearby, she created the aptly named Banyan Plaza, which is accessible from all sides and recreates the experience of communal gatherings beneath the trees.
This biomimicry brings tangible benefits: natural ventilation replaces air conditioning, minimising the centre’s carbon footprint, and the south of the plaza opens onto the park’s central lawn, creating an outdoor theatre for up to 30,000 people. It is a vivid demonstration of how state-of-the-art architecture and heritage greenery can be integrated in aesthetic and welcoming ways.
Besides new urban parks, the city has also been deliberate in sprucing up many of Kaohsiung’s old neighbourhoods. Canals, for instance, have been visually softened with trees and shrubs, helping to turn functional thoroughfares into serene inviting spaces.

Economic restructuring
Kaohsiung’s fourth and ongoing transformation is economic. The city forms a key part of the Southern Semiconductor Corridor: Taiwan’s plan to turn the southern part of the island, also including Tainan and Chiayi, into a hub for artificial intelligence research and chip manufacturing.
Already, the Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is building a plant to produce advanced two-nanometre chips. Nvidia, in partnership with Hon Hai Technology Group (Foxconn), is constructing a cutting-edge AI computing centre and setting up its Asian R&D headquarters in the city. Cisco, meanwhile, is helping to turn Kaohsiung Port into a “smart port” powered by AI and the Internet of Things.
This economic restructuring mirrors, and reinforces, Kaohsiung’s physical transformation – from a congested, polluted industrial city at the turn of the century into an attractive, smart and green one.
Donald Low is a Senior Lecturer and Professor of Practice, and Director of Leadership and Public Policy Executive Education at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology.

data.title: From flights of fancy to reality, National Games paves way for a bright future
data.description:
Several months ago, a group of business leaders in the city floated the idea that Hong Kong might play a key role in bringing the 2036 Olympics to the Greater Bay Area.
At the time it felt more than a little fanciful. The sort of pie in the sky thinking designed to start conversations around improving the city’s sporting infrastructure which, Kai Tak Sports Park aside, is badly in need of rejuvenation, and driving ambition, rather than being a proposal for serious consideration.
How times change. The National Games over the past two weeks have shown it to not only be an idea worthy of further discussion, but one now endorsed by former International Olympic Committee president Thomas Bach.
Bach told the region to “go for it”, suggesting there existed the constituent ingredients sprinkled among co-hosts Guangdong, Hong Kong and Macau to not only make a bid worthwhile but realistic.
Even if those were the words of a consummate politician, ones also whispered sweetly into South African ears last year, that does not mean they should be taken lightly.

Certainly the cooperation across the GBA has shown that staging large sporting events is well within the capabilities of all three hosts. In many ways it has been an unqualified success – there remains only the need for less encumbered travel across borders to be addressed for next time.
For Hong Kong there has also been the historic nature of the performances of its athletes to celebrate. The record 19 medals collected in the sea, the pool, on the cycling track, on the road, the piste and the tennis court more than justify the belief that the city’s size should not place a ceiling on any of its sporting targets.
Earlier this year, after a long break and a back injury, there were doubts over whether Siobhan Haughey would even make it to the Games. Her response was two golds and then two bronze, those final medals coming in the space of five unforgettable minutes.
Ceci Lee Sze-wing, meanwhile, has now won more gold, three, at a single National Games than any other city athlete and silver or better at the 2028 LA Olympics wouldn’t just put her in the conversation with Sarah Lee Wai-sze as the city’s best cyclist ever, but in a league of her own.
There are gaps, of course. Significant ones when it comes to track and field and gymnastics, two hugely important Olympic sports that Hong Kong has simply failed to get better at, in the case of the former, for decades.
Development, at least at the top level, appears to be in neither sport’s vocabulary. Fixing that should not be an insurmountable problem given the large sums of money the government hands to the Hong Kong Sports Institute every year – specifically for its Elite Athletes Development Fund – although getting officials there or at the relevant government bodies to accept responsibility for solving it could prove harder.
But solve it they must, because if Hong Kong harbours designs on joining the world’s elite when it comes to hosting events – and there should be no limit to that ambition – then its sport, all of it, has to match that.
Culturally, that may require a shift too, although hopefully parents have seen, and allowed their children to be inspired by, those sons and daughters who were not necessarily academically minded but have shone over the past fortnight.
The opportunity these Games have presented for the GBA cannot be underestimated, even if they should be qualified with a note of caution. Hosting the country’s best in a domestic event is one thing, bringing the world to your doorstep quite another.
Any Olympics is a goal that should be pursued, but maybe an Asian Games first, just to give everyone a bit more time to get up to speed on and off the track.

data.title: Mexico arrests 7 bodyguards of assassinated mayor over ‘aggravated homicide’
data.description:
Authorities on Friday arrested seven bodyguards suspected of being involved in the assassination earlier this month of a popular mayor in west-central Mexico whom they were supposed to be protecting.
At least five of the suspects were seen being arrested by state and federal authorities in Uruapan on Friday, steps away from the site where Mayor Carlos Manzo was killed during Day of the Dead festivities on November 1.
Later, the Michoacan state prosecutor’s office said in a brief statement that seven public officials, whom it did not identify, were arrested for “their likely participation in aggravated homicide”.
An official familiar with the operation said all seven had been bodyguards for Manzo. The official spoke on condition of anonymity because they were not authorised to speak publicly about it.
Soldiers, National Guard troops and state agents led the suspects out of a city building on Friday beside the central plaza where a teenage gunman had shot Manzo. Attention had immediately turned towards Manzo’s large security detail that had failed to stop the lone gunman, and suspicion only grew when it appeared that the gunman had been shot dead by members of the security detail after they had him on the ground.

On Wednesday, Mexican authorities announced the arrest of a man they said was involved in planning and ordering the killing. They connected the plot to the powerful Jalisco New Generation Cartel, one of the organised crime groups operating in Michoacan.
Manzo’s wife was sworn in as Uruapan’s mayor following his killing.
On Friday, members of Manzo’s – and now his wife’s – team recorded the arrests, some while crying as they watched colleagues being hauled off. Two people who spoke on condition of anonymity because they feared for their safety said that among those arrested were members of Manzo’s inner circle.
Uruapan’s central plaza is still filled with candles in tribute to the slain politician, who had been outspoken against organised crime’s grip. Notes expressing sadness about Manzo’s slaying and demanding justice still hang around the plaza.
The mayor’s killing brought renewed pressure on President Claudia Sheinbaum to adjust her security strategy. Days later she announced Plan Michoacan, which while heavy on spending for social programmes, also included having 10,000 troops spread out across the state to get control of the various criminal organisations operating there.

data.title: ‘Highly dangerous’: China’s UN envoy pours fresh scorn on ‘unrepentant’ Takaichi
data.description:
China’s ambassador to the United Nations has doubled down on his criticism of Japan, calling Japanese leader Sanae Takaichi’s earlier comments on Taiwan “highly dangerous” and damaging to the post-war international order.
According to state broadcaster CCTV, Fu Cong said in a letter to UN Secretary General Antonio Guterres on Friday that Japan was “unrepentant” for the “blatantly provocative” remarks of its prime minister.
He said Takaichi’s comments marked the first time a Japanese leader had “expressed its ambition for armed intervention on the Taiwan issue and the first time [Japan] has issued a military threat against China, openly challenging China’s core interests”.
“These remarks are extremely erroneous, highly dangerous, and have an extremely negative impact,” Fu wrote.
The letter, the CCTV report said, would be sent to all United Nations member states as an official document of the UN General Assembly.


Tensions between Tokyo and Beijing have risen sharply this month since Takaichi suggested that Japan could deploy its military forces in the event of a conflict in the Taiwan Strait. It was the first time a sitting Japanese prime minister had made such remarks and a departure from Japan’s long-held strategic ambiguity.
Even though the Japanese leader later clarified that her comments were only “hypothetical”, she has refused Beijing’s demands for her to retract them, saying her comments did not represent a change in Japan’s policy towards Taiwan.
Beijing has framed the comments as a sign of Tokyo’s desire to return to its pre-war path of militarism.
On Friday, Chinese foreign ministry spokeswoman Mao Ning said the remarks “eroded the political foundation of China-Japan relations and triggered outrage and condemnation from the Chinese people”.
“China will resolutely respond should anyone trample on the red line when it comes to the Taiwan question,” she said.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the United States and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
In the letter, Fu said Japan “remains unrepentant and refuses to retract its erroneous remarks” despite strong protests from China, adding that Beijing had expressed its dissatisfaction and opposition to this.
“Takaichi’s remarks seriously violated international law and the basic norms of international relations, severely undermined the post-war international order, and constituted a blatant provocation against the more than 1.4 billion Chinese people and the people of Asian countries that suffered from Japanese aggression,” he said, according to the CCTV report.
“As a defeated nation in World War II, Japan must deeply reflect on its historical crimes, abide by its political commitments regarding the Taiwan issue, immediately cease its provocations and transgression and retract its erroneous statements.”


Earlier in the week during debate about reforms to the 15-member UN Security Council, Fu said Japan was highly unqualified to become a permanent member of the group.
“In light of Sanae Takaichi’s atrocious behaviour, how can the international community trust that Japan will adhere to its commitment to a peaceful path?” Fu said, describing the Japanese leader’s comments on Taiwan as “extremely mistaken”.
“And how can they trust that it can bear the responsibility of maintaining international peace and security?
“A country like this is totally unqualified to demand a permanent seat on the Security Council.”
The criticism is part of Beijing’s ramped up attacks on Tokyo, which includes a warning to its citizens to avoid travel to Japan.
China is one of the biggest sources of foreign tourists for Japan and, in issuing the travel advisory, Chinese officials cited a series of targeted attacks.
However, the notice was largely viewed as a retaliatory move.
According to Japanese media, Japan’s foreign ministry dismissed the Chinese claims on Friday, saying China had made it seem “as if there has been a surge in criminal cases against Chinese nationals in Japan this year … however such an assertion is incorrect”.

data.title: Singapore minister apologises to Hong Kong government after football ‘idiots’ insult
data.description:
David Neo, the acting Singapore sports minister, has privately apologised to the Hong Kong government after he was captured on camera labelling the city’s football players and fans “idiots” following this week’s Asian Cup qualifier at Kai Tak Stadium.
Singapore won 2-1 to reach the 2027 finals at the expense of Hong Kong.
An Instagram live stream from one of the visiting players’ accounts showed Neo, the city state’s acting minister for culture, community and youth, telling a jubilant away dressing room: “It was fantastic … you kena [Malay for hit] pressured by them, all the fans were bloody idiots, end up players also played like idiots [sic] … But you all played like lions.”
Appointed to parliament in May, Neo quickly responded to an Instagram user who had called his comments “out of order”, although he stopped short of apologising.
“Indeed I probably should have been more respectful,” he said. “I take back what I said. The Hong Kong team were really tough and their fans were fully behind them. That’s something we should respect, and let’s also get behind our Lions.”

Nonetheless, Neo has privately said sorry to Rosanna Law Shuk-pui, the Hong Kong sports minister, according to a spokeswoman for the government’s Culture, Sports and Tourism Bureau.
“Singapore’s Acting Minister for Culture, Community and Youth has not only publicly apologised and retracted his remarks, but has also expressed his apologies via email to the Secretary for Culture, Sports and Tourism of the Hong Kong SAR Government,” said the spokeswoman.
“The Secretary [Law] stated that the incident will not affect the long-standing relationship between Hong Kong and Singapore in the fields of culture and sports.”
Singapore stunned a highly charged Kai Tak crowd of 47,762 after they mustered two second-half goals to cancel out Matt Orr’s 15th-minute opener.
Their players and around 1,000 travelling supporters celebrated wildly at full time, after Singapore had secured Asian Cup qualification for the first time. Their only previous finals appearance was as hosts in 1984.
The result has had significant ramifications for Hong Kong. A number of players are the wrong side of 30 and unlikely to have another shot at going to a major tournament, while the Post has been told that Ashley Westwood’s 15-month reign as head coach is poised to come to an end.

data.title: Hong Kong’s new openings, from the East Kowloon Cultural Centre to a new pasta bar
data.description:
Twist Pasta Bar

From the minds behind Honky Tonks Tavern and Mendel’s, the city’s latest pasta bar can be found in the form of Twist, with its made-for-Instagram interiors and hearty dishes such as bone marrow macaroni, a twist on Kau Kee’s famous curry brisket, and squid ink spaghetti with dried shrimp roe from Yau Kee. Beyond pasta, desserts such as the white chocolate and soy milk panna cotta pay homage to the local pudding tofu fa.
17 Bridges Street, Central
MoA Dining

Formerly known as Hue, the Woolly Pig group’s venue at the Hong Kong Museum of Art reopens as MoA Dining, pairing harbour views with modern Mediterranean fare. The Tsim Sha Tsui hotspot nods to local flavours with dishes such as a Hong Kong-style bouillabaisse alongside suckling pig and paella. The bright, art-filled room (look for works by Tracey Emin, Damien Hirst and Rocco Ritchie) also features an artisanal bread trolley.
1/F, Hong Kong Museum of Art, 10 Salisbury Road, Tsim Sha Tsui
La Baia

Bringing Mediterranean coastal flair to Kai Tak Sports Park, La Baia is designed to transport diners to the likes of Palma and Mykonos with its woven fabric installations, earthy tones and handwoven blue and brown seating. Offering rotating seasonal dishes, Baia’s menu showcases freshly made pizzas, pastas and classic antipasti such as calamari fritti and sea urchin aglio olio.
G/F, Dining Cove, Kai Tak Sports Park, Kai Tak
East Kowloon Cultural Centre

Designed by architect Rocco Yim Sen-kee, the East Kowloon Cultural Centre ushers in a new chapter for Hong Kong’s arts scene with five cutting-edge venues, including The Hall, with 1,200 seats, and The Theatre, with 536 seats, both of which are designed for large-scale and immersive productions. The debut season features 17 programmes, including the original Cantonese musical HighLights: The Memories of Charles Kao and internationally acclaimed acts such as Lucie in The Sky and Alt-Escape.
60 Ngau Tau Kok Road, Kowloon

data.title: Could Osaka be Japan’s second capital? Takaichi appears to demur on ally’s plan
data.description:
When Japan’s then incoming leader Sanae Takaichi was scrambling in October for a new coalition partner so the Liberal Democratic Party could remain in power, she seemed open to requests put forth by the Japan Innovation Party in return for its support.
One of the key demands of the JIP was that Osaka should be officially elevated in status to the nation’s “second capital”, with additional infrastructure for the city to enable it to become the seat of government in the event of a natural disaster crippling Tokyo.
Hirofumi Yoshimura, governor of Osaka prefecture since 2019 and head of the JIP for nearly a year, emerged from a meeting with senior LDP leaders in October and declared that Takaichi had said she would be “willing to work” with him on the “second capital” plan.
While the two parties have begun discussions on the idea and there are vague suggestions on how to turn Osaka into Japan’s second capital, the JIP is doing all the running. And questions about the feasibility – and desirability – of the plan are quietly being asked.
With Takaichi cemented now as prime minister, analysts say she appears to have gone cool on the idea.
And that has left the JIP in a delicate position on whether it should stick with the alliance and hope the LDP would be aligned with the plan in future, or walk away from the ruling party if it is getting cold feet.

Despite the JIP’s dominant position in the city, it has lost two referendums – in 2015 and again in 2020 – after failing to get support from voters for its “Osaka Metropolis Plan”, as part of its ambition to position the city as the nation’s second capital.
If implemented, this plan would have led to the dissolution of Osaka City as a single municipal entity and reorganisation of its 24 administrative wards into a few large, semi-autonomous special wards, similar to the system in Tokyo.
“This debate has been going on for some time in Osaka already, but the JIP has already failed with votes previously,” said Hiromi Murakami, a professor of political science at the Tokyo campus of Temple University.
“The results were very close each time, but the people of Osaka rejected a proposal that is in many ways the party’s raison d’etre,” she told This Week in Asia. The JIP’s hope, she added, was that an alliance with the LDP might give the proposal the impetus required to get it over the line.
The first referendum fell short by less than 1 per cent of the votes cast, with the plan defeated in the second referendum by a similarly narrow 1.2 per cent margin.
Voters were not convinced by the JIP’s claims that the change would streamline the bloated bureaucracy by eliminating overlapping layers at the city and local level, reducing costs and enhancing efficiency.
Opponents of the capital plan – which included Osaka’s LDP chapter acting in concert with the Japanese Communist Party – successfully argued that the proposed changes would cause revenue shortfalls, worsen local services, especially in less well-off parts of the city, and centralise power and decision-making at the prefecture level to the detriment of local administrations.

“The JIP really wanted this from the LDP because if they were able to go back to their supporters and say they had achieved their goal, that they had done what they set out to do and that the party could then build on this, then that would have been a success,” Murakami said.
“And Takaichi and the LDP were obviously not as keen on the plan, but they agreed to it in return for the coalition. But with the LDP backing away, the only conclusion is that this is a disaster for the JIP. They have lost a lot of face.”
Murakami pointed out that the LDP had “never been the party of reform”, and it traditionally would not back measures calling for smaller bureaucracies.
Stephen Nagy, a professor of international relations at Tokyo’s International Christian University, said the LDP had other motivations to go back on its word.
“The JIP is very much a regional party and it has a vision of a more decentralised Japan that will contribute to the economic growth of Osaka,” he said. “There is also the symbolism of moving some of the power out of Tokyo. None of that appeals to the LDP.”
Osaka is traditionally a more liberal city, and the LDP would resist any political manoeuvres that might weaken its control or grant additional powers to another party, even one that is an ally, according to Nagy.
“There is a clear political component here and the LDP has no intention of diluting its own powers.”

Conservative-leaning media outlets have poured cold water on the JIP’s capital plan, with an editorial on November 19 in the Yomiuri newspaper dismissing it as “difficult to understand”.
In response to the JIP’s suggestions put forward in its discussions with the LDP, the Yomiuri said its proposal “appears designed to favour Osaka prefecture, the party’s home turf”. It also criticised the JIP’s call for infrastructure development, suggesting it “brought a sense of discomfort”.
Yomiuri also dismissed the argument for Osaka to be a backup for Tokyo in the event of a natural disaster, saying that the city faced the possibility of a major earthquake in the offshore Nankai Trough of between 60 per cent and 90 per cent in the next 30 years.
“To disperse the risks from disasters, it does not seem necessary to limit relocation sites for ministries and agencies to a major city. This is likely an issue to be considered separately from the matter of the second capital.”

data.title: Frida Kahlo’s self-portrait, sold for US$54.7m, breaks auction record for female artists
data.description:
A haunting 1940 self-portrait by famed Mexican artist Frida Kahlo sold Thursday for US$54.7 million and became the top-selling work by any female artist at an auction.
The painting of Kahlo asleep in a bed — titled “El sueno (La cama)” or in English, “The Dream (The Bed)” — surpassed the record held by Georgia O’Keeffe’s “Jimson Weed/White Flower No. 1,” which sold for US$44.4 million in 2014.
The sale at Sotheby’s in New York also topped Kahlo’s own auction record for a work by a Latin American artist. The 1949 painting “Diego and I,” depicting the artist and her husband, muralist Diego Rivera, went for US$34.9 million in 2021. Her paintings are reported to have sold privately for even more.
The self-portrait is among the few Kahlo pieces that have remained in private hands outside Mexico, where her body of work has been declared an artistic monument. Her works in both public and private collections within the country cannot be sold abroad or destroyed.
The painting comes from a private collection, whose owner has not been disclosed, and is legally eligible for international sale. Some art historians have scrutinized the sale for cultural reasons, while others have raised concern that the painting — last exhibited publicly in the late 1990s — could again disappear from public view after the auction. It has already been requested for upcoming exhibitions in cities including New York, London and Brussels.
The buyer’s identity was not disclosed.
The piece depicts Kahlo asleep in a wooden, colonial-style bed that floats in the clouds. She is draped in a golden blanket and entangled in crawling vines and leaves. Above the bed lies a skeleton figure wrapped in dynamite.

Kahlo vibrantly and unsparingly depicted herself and events from her life, which was upended by a bus accident at 18. She started to paint while bedridden, underwent a series of painful surgeries on her damaged spine and pelvis, then wore casts until her death in 1954 at age 47.
During the years Kahlo was confined to her bed, she came to view it as a bridge between worlds as she explored her mortality.
Before the auction, her great-niece, Mara Romeo Kahlo, celebrated the significance of the upcoming sale during a recent interview with The Associated Press in Mexico City.
“I’m very proud that she’s one of the most valued women, because really, what woman doesn’t identify with Frida, or what person doesn’t?” she said. “I think everyone carries a little piece of my aunt in their heart.”
The painting was the star of a sale of more than 100 surrealist works by artists including Salvador Dalí, René Magritte, Max Ernst and Dorothea Tanning.
Kahlo resisted being labeled a surrealist, a style of art that’s dreamlike and centers on a fascination with the unconscious mind.
“I never painted dreams,” she once said. “I painted my own reality.”
In its catalog note, Sotheby’s said the painting “offers a spectral meditation on the porous boundary between sleep and death.”
“The suspended skeleton is often interpreted as a visualization of her anxiety about dying in her sleep, a fear all too plausible for an artist whose daily existence was shaped by chronic pain and past trauma,” the catalog notes.

Earlier this week, a Gustav Klimt painting that helped save the life of its Jewish subject during the Holocaust sold at Sotheby’s for US$236.4 million. Klimt’s “Portrait of Elisabeth Lederer” became one of the most expensive pieces of artwork ever sold at auction, second only to Leonardo da Vinci’s “Salvator Mundi” at US$450 million — the record-holder over all and among male artists.

data.title: K-drama casting news about Shin Hye-sun, Na In-woo, Ji Chang-wook and more
data.description:
A cross-cultural romance, a fantastical connection, a gripping historical drama and a crime-comedy-mystery are among the latest projects attracting Korea’s top acting talent.
Here’s the K-drama casting round-up for this week.
1. 1/24
Shin Hye-sun and Na In-woo have been approached to appear in a new romantic comedy together. They are mulling offers to appear in 1/24, a Netflix original series based on the webtoon An Hour of Romance.
The story follows a male tennis ace and a female reality show producer, both harbouring emotional scars, who experience a fantastical event that sees them swap bodies for one hour each day. While navigating this phenomenon, the pair begin to learn about one another, eventually falling in love.

Known for the hit period drama Mr. Queen, Shin recently starred in Welcome to Samdal-ri and Dear Hyeri. She has two coming shows: the Netflix drama The Art of Sarah alongside Lee Joon-hyuk, and the tvN series Secret Audit with Gong Myung.
Shin and Na have worked together before, as Na also appeared in Mr. Queen when he was an emerging actor. He has since become a leading star, known for shows like Jinxed at First and Marry My Husband.
Directing the show will be Kim Sang-hyub, known for the previous hit webtoon adaptation True Beauty and who most recently worked on Melancholia.
2. Merry Berry Love
Currently on screens in The Manipulated, Ji Chang-wook is set to appear in yet another Disney+ production, although this new project will mark a departure from the thrillers he has been making with the House of Mouse.

Merry Berry Love is a bright romantic comedy that will be co-produced by Korea’s CJ ENM and Japan’s Nippon TV, and will see Ji team up with rising Japanese actress Mio Imada.
Set on a remote island, the series will follow Korean spatial designer Lee Yoo-bin (played by Ji) and Japanese strawberry farmer Karin Shirahama (Imada).
In addition to Merry Berry Love, Ji is also set to join forces with Jun Ji-hyun for the high-profile fantasy romantic comedy Human X Gumiho. First, Ji will appear in the Netflix drama Scandals alongside Son Ye-jin and Nana.

Imada is known for the Tokyo Revengers films and the recent drama Trillion Game in Japan.
Kim Soo-jung, known for the hit BL (boy love) drama Semantic Error, will direct, while The Divorce Insurance scribe Lee Jae-yoon will pen the script.
3. Munmu
Lee Hyun-wook (Mine), Jang Hyuk (Family: The Unbreakable Bond) and Kim Kang-woo (The Tyrant) are set to headline the period drama Munmu, which will follow the formation of the Unified Silla Dynasty (668-935).

Lee will play Kim Pom-min, the Silla Prince and strategist who would become known as Munmu, the unifier of the three kingdoms of Silla, Goguryeo and Baekje. Jang will play Yeon Gaesomun, the dictatorial leader of Goguryeo, while Kim will appear as Pom-min’s father, the Silla king Kim Chun-chu.
Also tapped to appear are Park Sung-woong (Bloodhounds) as Silla general Kim Yu-sin; Jung Woong-in (Trigger) as a veiled political power within the Silla court; and Cho Sung-ha (Queen Mantis) as Goguryeo war hero Go Geon-mu, who returns from war to peacefully rule Goguryeo before a bloody coup.
The series will be brought to life by Kim Yeong-jo, who previously served as a co-director on the period epic Hwarang: The Poet Warrior Youth.
4. Fifties Professionals
Shin Ha-kyun (Beyond Evil), Oh Jung-se (It’s Okay to Not Be Okay) and Heo Sung-tae (Squid Game season 1) are confirmed as the leads of the coming MBC drama Fifties Professionals.

The series follows a trio of middle-aged men who, long after their glory days, were exiled to remote Yeongseon Island due to a mysterious incident. But then events conspire to bring them back into action as they band together to uncover the mystery that occurred 10 years ago.
Shin will play Jung Ho-myung, a former black agent at the National Intelligence Service who was framed for a crime he did not commit and now works as a chef in a Chinese restaurant.
Oh is slated to play North Korean special agent Bong Je-soon, who has lost his memory. Heo will feature as former gangland kingpin Kang Beom-ryong, who now runs a convenience store.
Fifties Professionals will be directed by Han Dong-hwa (Shadow Detective) and written by Jang Won-seob. The series is expected to launch in the first half of 2026.
